title: The resort named the road
body: Flamingo Road takes its name from the resort at Las Vegas Boulevard. Several Strip cross streets were renamed for casinos in the late 1950s and early 1960s.

title: The casino is gone; the corner remains
body: The Tropicana closed in April 2024 after welcoming guests for 67 years. Its two hotel towers were leveled in an elaborate implosion that October.

title: Where Las Vegas officially stops
body: At Las Vegas Boulevard, Sahara Avenue marks the boundary of the City of Las Vegas. Most of the resort corridor to the south is in unincorporated Clark County.

title: The Blue Angel watches Five Points
body: Near Charleston, Eastern, and Fremont stands the restored 15-foot Blue Angel from a 1957 motel. Betty Willis, who created the Welcome to Las Vegas sign, designed it.

title: Two big parks, one west-side line
body: All American Park sits near Buffalo and Charleston, while Centennial Hills Park anchors Buffalo farther north. The two large green spaces make opposite ends of the road easier to remember.

title: Angel Park marks the crossing
body: Durango passes Angel Park near Charleston, Durango Hills Park farther north, and Thunderbird Family Sports Complex near Ann. Their names turn one long west-side road into a chain of park markers.

title: UNLV began beside a dirt road
body: UNLV opened beside Maryland Parkway in 1957, when the road was still two lanes of dirt. The original campus occupied just 80 acres north of this crossing.

title: Three roads frame one giant park
body: Sunset forms the park's northern edge, Warm Springs forms its southern edge, and Eastern borders it on the west. Together they make a three-road landmark beside the airport.

title: Before the freeway, this was the connection
body: Boulder Highway was once the only road linking downtown Las Vegas, Henderson, and Boulder City. Motels, casinos, and roadside businesses grew along it before US 95 drew traffic away.

title: Stephanie is a real person
body: Stephanie Street was named for Stephanie Page Wurzer when she was a child and the route was still an isolated dirt road. It later became one of Henderson's major commercial streets.

title: Roy Rogers had a ranch here
body: Cowboy star Roy Rogers bought Warm Springs Ranch after visiting Las Vegas for Helldorado. The ranch and its spring were southwest of today's Paradise and Warm Springs intersection.

title: The Welcome sign is just south
body: Betty Willis designed the Welcome to Fabulous Las Vegas sign in 1959. It stands in the Las Vegas Boulevard median just south of Russell Road.
